title: An Introduction to Jesus' Life
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
series: The First Year of Jesus' Ministry
source_files: An Introduction to Jesus’ Life - Mark 1_1-8 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Introduction: Why Study the Life of Christ?
subpoints: Mark calls the entire life of Christ 'the gospel of Jesus Christ' — this tells us how important it is to know the life of Christ
When we love someone, we want to know everything about them; because we love Jesus, we will spend the first semester of 2023 considering the first year of his public ministry
The series will primarily cover John 1 through 4 (the year of obscurity) with material from Matthew, Mark, and Luke throughout

Seven hopes for the series: know Jesus better, appreciate him more, embrace his teaching, believe in him as Savior, strengthen faith in the reliability of the Gospels, proclaim the good news, and glorify God

outline.title: Historical Context: When Did Jesus Live?
subpoints: Jesus was born in approximately 5 BC — Dionysius Exiguus (AD 525) proposed basing history on Christ but miscalculated the year by about five years
The Gregorian calendar (1582, Pope Gregory XIII) corrected the Julian calendar's seasonal drift but retained the incorrect year-one placement
John the Baptist began his ministry in early AD 26; Jesus was baptized in fall/winter of AD 26; Jesus was crucified in spring of AD 30
Jewish reckoning of time is not precise by Western standards — portions of three years would be counted as three years
The three years of public ministry are established by counting the Passover feasts in the Gospel of John — the most chronologically ordered of the four Gospels

outline.title: The Meaning of 'Gospel' in Roman and Jewish Contexts
subpoints: In Roman culture, 'gospel' (euangelion) meant joyful tidings or joyful news, and referred to a historical event that introduced a new situation in the world
In Jewish context, 'gospel' meant good news that the promises of God were about to be fulfilled — the faithfulness of God bringing his promises to pass
Combining both: the gospel is 'joyful news that the promises of God are about to be fulfilled in a way that will change the world'
The term does not primarily refer to a book but to a living proclamation from the lips of God's messengers — which is now us
The gospel of Jesus Christ is not just about his death and resurrection but about his entire life — all of Mark 1 through 16 is good news

outline.title: Jesus Is the Son of God: What That Means
subpoints: Mark sandwiches his Gospel with the title 'Son of God': it appears in verse 1 and at the crucifixion where the centurion declares 'Truly this was the Son of God' (Mark 15:39)
The religious leaders of Jesus's day understood that 'Son of God' was a claim to equality with God — which is why they sought to kill him (John 5:17-23)

Being the Son of God means equal in nature (of the same essence as God), equal in power (authority to raise the dead), equal in authority (the Father has given all judgment to the Son), and equal in glory (John 17:5)
The proclamation that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of the living God is the rock upon which Jesus promised to build his church (Matthew 16:16-18)
The gates of hell will not prevail against a church built on this confession — not on Peter, but on the proclamation itself

outline.title: John the Baptist: The Gospel Begins with His Ministry
subpoints: According to Mark, the gospel of Jesus Christ inseparably begins with the ministry of John the Baptist — Peter confirms this in Acts 1:21-22
John's title 'the Baptist' refers to what he was known for (the one who baptized), not to a theological persuasion — he would better be called John the Baptizer, or 'John the Presbyterian'
Mark quotes both Malachi 3:1 and Isaiah 40:3, referring to the combined citation simply as 'the prophets' or 'Isaiah' — an ancient practice of citing the larger book
The quotation shows that Mark sees the gospel as the fulfillment of all the promises God had given Israel — the Baptist is the forerunner of Malachi, the voice in the wilderness of Isaiah
John's ministry: baptism in the wilderness, preaching repentance, crowds from Jerusalem and all Judea, camel-hair clothing and leather belt (echoing Elijah), locusts and wild honey

outline.title: The Gospel as Historical Fact
subpoints: The gospel of Jesus Christ is inseparably tied to historical facts: Christ died (historical), for our sins (theological), was buried (historical), was raised (historical), appeared to the Twelve, to five hundred, to James, to Paul (historical)
Paul says in 1 Corinthians 15: some who saw the risen Christ are still alive — go talk to them; this is verifiable history
Our faith is a propositional faith — it is reasonable and based on facts; knowing God and knowing *about* God cannot be separated
Jesus was a real person, not an idea; he lived, died, rose from the dead, and now lives at the right hand of the Father
Mark uses the historical present tense almost exclusively — rare use of past tense — so that the reader is drawn into the narrative and lives it out with Christ

summary:
Launching a new series on the first year of Jesus' public ministry — primarily covering John 1 through 4 with material from Matthew, Mark, and Luke — Pastor Tito Lyro begins at Mark 1:1 to examine the foundational claim: this is the gospel of Jesus Christ, the Son of God. The sermon provides essential historical orientation: Jesus was born around 5 BC (Dionysius Exiguus miscalculated by five years in AD 525), his three years of public ministry run from approximately AD 26 to AD 30, and the most chronologically reliable Gospel is John, which enables scholars to establish the timeline from the number of Passovers it records.

The word 'gospel' is unpacked in both its Roman cultural sense (joyful tidings that change the world) and its Jewish theological sense (good news that the promises of God are about to be fulfilled). Together these yield the sermon's working definition: the gospel is joyful news that the promises of God are about to be fulfilled in a way that will change the world. Importantly, the gospel of Jesus Christ is not merely his death and resurrection but the entire story of his life — everything Mark records is good news.

The sermon also develops what the title 'Son of God' means. The religious leaders of Jesus's day understood it as a claim to equality with God, which is why they sought to kill him (John 5:17-23). The title means equal in nature, power, authority, and glory with the Father. It is on the confession that Jesus is the Son of the living God that Christ promised to build his church (Matthew 16). The sermon closes by noting that Mark inseparably ties the gospel of Christ to the preparatory ministry of John the Baptist, who is the fulfillment of both the Malachi forerunner promise and the Isaiah voice-in-the-wilderness promise.
transcript:
## Scripture Reading: Mark 1:1-8

If you are able, would you remain standing for a moment longer? We are turning to the Gospel of Mark chapter 1. Starting a new series today that should take us to the end of May. Mark chapter 1, verses 1 through 8.

*The beginning of the gospel of Jesus Christ, the Son of God. As it is written in the prophets: 'Behold, I send My messenger before Your face, who will prepare Your way before You.' 'The voice of one crying in the wilderness: Prepare the way of the LORD; make His paths straight.' John came baptizing in the wilderness and preaching a baptism of repentance for the remission of sins. Then all the land of Judea, and those from Jerusalem, went out to him and were all baptized by him in the Jordan River, confessing their sins. Now John was clothed with camel's hair and with a leather belt around his waist, and he ate locusts and wild honey. And he preached, saying, 'There comes One after me who is mightier than I, whose sandal strap I am not worthy to stoop down and loose. I indeed baptized you with water, but He will baptize you with the Holy Spirit.'*

This is the Word of our Lord. Let us pray together. Father, we pray that you speak to us as we consider this portion of your Word. We pray that you help me to proclaim what is true and what is helpful. We pray these things in Jesus' name. Amen. Please be seated.

## Why Study the Life of Christ?

Mark tells us in verse 1 that he is writing about the gospel of Jesus Christ, and then he proceeds to tell us the whole story of Christ's life. He tells us all the facts about the life of Christ and the theological significance of those facts. The fact that Mark calls the entire life of Christ 'the gospel of Christ' tells us how important it is for us to know that life.

In addition to that, when we love someone, we want to know everything about them. And we love Jesus. Because of that, we are going to spend most of the first semester of 2023 considering the first year of Jesus's life — which should take us through John chapter 4 with a sprinkling of Matthew, Mark, and Luke in there somewhere. That is the plan through the end of May.

Now, I want to tell you what my hope is for this series — seven things I hope will happen:

**First:** I hope you get to know Jesus better. I mean real facts about Jesus. We tend in our culture to value experiences over knowledge — it doesn't matter what you know, it's what you experience. That is not a Christian way of thinking. Our faith is a propositional faith. It is a reasonable faith. It can be reasoned based on facts. When Paul summarized the gospel in 1 Corinthians 15, he listed historical facts one after another: Christ died — historical fact. Was buried — historical fact. Rose from the dead — historical fact. Appeared to the twelve, to five hundred, to James, to Paul — go talk to them; some are still alive. Our faith is grounded in facts. And facts are how we know people.

**Second:** Through those facts, I hope you have a greater appreciation for Jesus himself — that your love for him grows as you learn more about him.

**Third:** I hope you embrace Jesus's teaching in his first year of public ministry and apply it to your life with the help of the Holy Spirit.

**Fourth:** If you have never believed in Jesus for the sake of your soul, I hope this series helps you know him as your Savior. I hope the Spirit grabs hold of your heart and gives you a heart of flesh to believe in Jesus Christ — that you stop playing church and actually become a follower, that you stop being a hypocrite and actually repent and believe.

**Fifth:** I hope our faith as a church is strengthened as we see how reliable and accurate the four Gospels are. As we go through them, you will see how they fit together perfectly. The evidence for them is insurmountable. Only the ignorant tries to deny it.

**Sixth:** I hope all of us will be better prepared to proclaim the good news concerning Jesus Christ. As we get to know him better, we will also be better prepared to tell others.

**Seventh, and above all:** I hope this series helps us glorify God and enjoy him now and forever, which is the goal of everything we do.

## Historical Context: When Did Jesus Live?

Jesus was born in approximately 5 BC. Now that might be a surprise — the year 5 *before* Christ? How could he be born five years before his own birth?

Well, in AD 525 — in the year 525 of our era — a Christian monk named Dionysius Exiguus suggested that the history of the world should be divided in relation to Christ: things before Christ would be called BC, and things after Christ would be called AD, from the Latin *anno Domini* — the year of the Lord. Europe and the Christian world adopted this framework, making the life of Christ the dividing factor of history. The problem is that in AD 525, with limited historical resources, Dionysius was off by approximately five years in his dating of Christ's birth.

We follow the Gregorian calendar, established in 1582 by Pope Gregory XIII, who reorganized the old Julian calendar because the Julian calendar was 360 days — and if enough years go by with a 360-day calendar, the seasons shift out of alignment. Gregory added leap years and so on, but he kept the year-one placement where it had been, in the wrong location. So that is the calendar we use today.

So: Jesus was born in approximately 5 BC. He had a three-year public ministry. Early in AD 26, John the Baptist began his ministry. Later in AD 26, in the fall or winter, Jesus was baptized by John. In the spring of AD 30, Jesus was crucified — approximately three years of public ministry.

Now an important note: the Jewish mind was not concerned with precision in the way the Western mind is. Time was more fluid. Three years and ten months would be counted as either three years or four years depending on how you count. So 'three years' is a general designation for the span of Jesus's ministry, not a count of exactly thirty-six months.

This series is only going to cover the first year of that ministry — the year of obscurity. You do not find a lot about it in the other Gospels, but John spends four full chapters on it. That is why I said this series will mostly be about John 1 through 4, with sprinkling from Matthew, Mark, and Luke.

## The Meaning of 'Gospel'

In Roman culture, which is the culture Mark was writing into — he likely wrote for the church in Rome in the mid to late 50s of the first century — the word 'gospel' (*euangelion*) meant joyful tidings or joyful news. It also referred to a historical event which introduced a new situation in the world. The word was often used for things the emperor did, because whatever the emperor did was a world-changing event — even if he just closed a door. So 'gospel' in Roman ears meant: good tidings about something that is going to change the world.

But Mark was also writing in a Jewish context. For the Jews, the word 'gospel' meant good news that the promises of God were going to be fulfilled — or were about to be fulfilled. It was about the faithfulness of God in bringing to pass all the things he had promised his people. We see that in how Mark begins: he quotes Malachi and Isaiah, showing that the arrival of Jesus and John the Baptist is the fulfillment of those ancient promises.

Putting those two things together: the word 'gospel' means joyful news that the promises of God are about to be fulfilled in a way that will change the world. And that news is to be proclaimed by God's messengers — which ends up being us.

We tend to think of 'gospel' as referring to a book. But that use of the word did not come about until the second century. In the first century it primarily refers to the living word of hope proclaimed by a messenger. This is the proclamation that the Christian makes.

And notice: the gospel that Mark is writing about is the gospel of Jesus Christ — not the gospel that belongs to Jesus, but the gospel that is about Jesus. Mark and the other Gospel writers tell us the story of Jesus, and the whole thing is good news. We tend to think the good news is only about Jesus dying for our sins and rising from the dead — and that is wonderful news. But the good news is more than that. Jesus also lived for us. And when Mark calls the entire life of Christ the gospel, it includes his birth, his childhood, his teaching, his miracles, his relationships, and everything else.

This also teaches us that there is no gospel apart from the historical account concerning Jesus Christ. Jesus was not an idea. He was a real person who lived, who died, who rose from the dead, and who now lives at the right hand of the Father. And sometimes we forget that Jesus is a real, living person — not just a historical character in a book.

Mark goes out of his way to use what is called the historical present tense. Mark rarely uses the past tense — almost everything is in the present — so that the reader is brought into the narrative and can live it out with Christ. That is the way Mark presents Jesus: as real, present, immediate.

## Jesus Is the Son of God

Notice also that Mark gives us one more identifying characteristic of Jesus in verse 1: *The beginning of the gospel of Jesus Christ, the Son of God.* Mark sandwiches his Gospel with that title. We find it at the very beginning, and we find it at the crucifixion, where the centurion — after all the signs that took place at the moment of Jesus's death — looks at Jesus and says: *Truly this Man was the Son of God* (Mark 15:39).

This was a favorite title of all the Gospel writers. And it is important because the truth that Jesus is the Son of God is a foundational truth for the church of Jesus Christ. You remember when Jesus found himself in the region of Caesarea Philippi, northeast of the Sea of Galilee — Gentile territory — and he had a conversation with his disciples alone. He asked: 'Who do people say that I am?' And they said: some say you are a prophet, Jeremiah, Elijah. But then he narrowed it: 'Who do *you* say that I am?' And Peter spoke: 'You are the Christ, the Son of the living God.' Jesus said: 'You are Peter — and upon this rock I will build my church, and the gates of hell will not prevail against it.'

The rock is not Peter. Grammatically it is impossible for the rock to be Peter in that sentence. The rock is the proclamation — that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of the living God. It is on this confession that Christ builds his church. And as we build the church on that proclamation, the gates of hell have no chance against it.

But what does it mean that Jesus is the Son of God? What it means is that Jesus is equal with God — that Jesus is God himself.

The religious leaders of Jesus's time understood this. In John 5, Jesus says: *My Father has been working until now, and I have been working.* Therefore the Jews sought to kill him — because not only did he break the Sabbath, but he said that God was his Father, making himself equal with God. The immediate audience, who knew the Old Testament, recognized that claiming to be the Son of God was claiming to be equal with God, of the same nature as God.

Being the Son of God means he was of the same nature as God — God himself. He was equal in power: as he continues in John 5, he says the Father has given him the power to raise people from the dead. He was equal in authority: the Father has given all judgment to the Son. And he was equal in glory: in John 17 he prays, 'Restore to me the glory I had with you before the foundation of the world.'

That is the gospel that Mark and all the other Gospel writers are presenting to us — joyful news about Jesus Christ the Son of God. That news will change the world. And that news is what we are going to spend the next few months considering together.

## John the Baptist: The Gospel Begins Here

According to Mark, the gospel of Jesus begins with the ministry of John the Baptist. One commentator said: 'The opening words emphasize that the good news concerning Jesus, the Christ, was inseparably bound up with the preparation provided by John the Baptist.' Peter also considered John's ministry as inseparable from Jesus's ministry. In Acts 1, when they are choosing a replacement for Judas, Peter says the replacement must be someone who witnessed the gospel of Christ beginning with the ministry of John through the resurrection and ascension of Christ.

A comment about the title 'the Baptist.' John the Son of Zacharias and Elizabeth — cousin of Jesus; Elizabeth was a cousin of Mary, making John and Jesus cousins — became known as John the Baptist. I have actually been asked: if John was a Baptist, why are you a Presbyterian? The title Baptist has nothing to do with theological persuasion. It refers to what he was known for: the one who baptized. A better title would be John the Baptizer. If we were giving him a title based on theological persuasion, we would call him John the Presbyterian.

Now, our translation says in verse 2 'as it is written in the prophets,' but if you have a different translation you will see 'as it is written in Isaiah.' That is actually a better reading. Mark quotes both Malachi 3:1 and Isaiah 40:3, but he refers to the citation simply as 'Isaiah' — following an ancient practice of citing the larger book. But we see from those two quotations that Mark viewed the gospel as the fulfillment of all the promises God had given his people. John the Baptist is both the forerunner of Malachi and the voice in the wilderness of Isaiah.

We will consider John's ministry in more detail next Lord's Day. For today, I just want us to sit with verse 1: *The beginning of the gospel of Jesus Christ, the Son of God.* The joyful news that the promises of God are being fulfilled in a way that will change the world — concerning Jesus Christ, who is God himself.

Father, thank you for your Word. We thank you for writing it down for us. We thank you for your Spirit who works in us to understand it. As we begin this series today, we pray that you guide us through it, that it would be profitable for your church, that we would grow in love with Jesus Christ. For those who do not believe in you, we pray that you change their hearts that they might believe, even as they hear the truth of Christ proclaimed to them. Revive us through the proclamation of your Word. In Jesus' name. Amen.
